description.en: World-renowned red wine from the Bordeaux region, known for its complex blends of Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Cabernet Franc

seo.metaTitle: Bordeaux Wine | France's Premier Red Wine Region Guide
seo.metaDescription: Explore Bordeaux wine — France's most celebrated wine region producing world-class reds from Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Cabernet Franc blends.

enhanced_description: Bordeaux wine is arguably the most prestigious and influential wine in the world, produced in the Bordeaux region of southwestern France along the banks of the Garonne, Dordogne, and Gironde rivers. The region encompasses over 111,000 hectares of vineyards, making it one of the largest fine wine-producing areas on Earth. Bordeaux wines are classified under a complex system of appellations d'origine controlee (AOC), with the most famous classification dating to 1855, when the top chateaux of the Medoc and Sauternes were ranked into five growths (crus) for the Paris Exposition. The Left Bank (Medoc, Graves, Pessac-Leognan) is dominated by Cabernet Sauvignon, producing structured, tannic wines built for aging, while the Right Bank (Saint-Emilion, Pomerol) favors Merlot, yielding softer, more approachable wines with plush fruit character. A typical Bordeaux blend combines multiple grape varieties, with each contributing distinct qualities: Cabernet Sauvignon provides structure and blackcurrant notes, Merlot adds body and roundness, Cabernet Franc contributes aromatic complexity and freshness, and small amounts of Petit Verdot and Malbec may add color and spice. The region also produces excellent dry whites from Sauvignon Blanc and Semillon, as well as the legendary sweet wines of Sauternes and Barsac. The concept of terroir is paramount in Bordeaux, where the gravel, clay, and limestone soils of different appellations impart unique characteristics to the wines. En primeur (wine futures) sales each spring are a major event in the global wine market, with critics and merchants tasting barrel samples to assess each new vintage.

detailed_ingredients.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
detailed_ingredients.purpose: Primary grape of the Left Bank, providing structure, tannins, and blackcurrant flavors
substitutes: Cabernet Franc as a lighter alternative

detailed_ingredients.name: Merlot
detailed_ingredients.purpose: Dominant grape of the Right Bank, contributing softness, plum fruit, and body

detailed_ingredients.name: Cabernet Franc
detailed_ingredients.purpose: Adds aromatic complexity with notes of violets, herbs, and raspberry
substitutes: Petit Verdot for color and spice

detailed_ingredients.name: Petit Verdot
detailed_ingredients.purpose: Used in small quantities for deep color, floral aromatics, and tannic backbone

cultural_significance.origin_story: Winemaking in Bordeaux dates back to Roman times, but the region's international reputation was cemented during English rule in the 12th-15th centuries, when 'claret' became the drink of the English court. The 1855 Classification, created for the Paris World Exhibition, established the hierarchy of Grands Crus Classes that still defines Bordeaux prestige today.
cultural_significance.cultural_importance: Bordeaux wine is deeply woven into French identity, gastronomy, and economy. The region's chateaux are architectural treasures, and wine culture shapes everything from local cuisine to social rituals. A bottle of Bordeaux is the standard gift when invited to a French dinner party.

variations.name: Left Bank Bordeaux (Medoc, Margaux, Pauillac, Saint-Julien)
variations.description: Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant blends with firm tannins, cassis, cedar, and exceptional aging potential

variations.name: Right Bank Bordeaux (Saint-Emilion, Pomerol)
variations.description: Merlot-dominant blends that are rounder and more approachable, with plum, truffle, and chocolate notes

variations.name: Bordeaux Blanc
variations.description: Dry white wines from Sauvignon Blanc and Semillon, ranging from crisp to richly oaked

variations.name: Sauternes
variations.description: Legendary sweet dessert wine made from botrytis-affected Semillon, Sauvignon Blanc, and Muscadelle grapes

best_restaurants: Le Chapon Fin (Bordeaux) — historic gastronomic restaurant with legendary wine cellar
Le Gabriel (Bordeaux) — two-Michelin-star dining in Place de la Bourse
La Grande Maison de Bernard Magrez (Bordeaux) — Pierre Gagnaire restaurant at a grand cru chateau
street_food_areas: Marche des Capucins (Bordeaux) — the city's beloved covered market
Les Halles de Bacalan — modern food hall by the Cite du Vin
Wine bars in the Saint-Pierre quarter of old Bordeaux

price_ranges.street_food: EUR 4-8 (glass of Bordeaux AOC at a wine bar)
price_ranges.restaurant: EUR 8-20 (glass of classified growth at a restaurant)
